nativeRange.habitat: Deep lagoon and seaward reefs with rich coral growth and high vertical relief; juveniles tend to be secretive on inshore reefs while adults are solitary or in small groups, typically at 15-40 m depth (range 2-250 m)

diet.notes: Generally accepts frozen, freeze-dried, fresh, and flake foods in captivity. In the wild has been documented feeding on coral polyps, benthic invertebrates, and algae (FishBase trophic level 3.6). A varied diet including meaty items such as mysis and finely chopped seafood alongside marine algae and vitamin-enriched preparations is commonly advised. Newly imported individuals can be initially shy feeders and tend to benefit from live or frozen enticement foods to begin feeding.

avoidWith: other Heniochus species unless introduced simultaneously

summary: The largest member of the bannerfish genus, reaching around 30 cm with bold black bands and canary-yellow accents. Generally considered peaceful and is typically housed in FOWLR systems; not regarded as reef safe owing to a documented appetite for coral polyps and tubeworms.
careNotes:
The Singular Bannerfish is the largest of the Heniochus group and an active swimmer, so a long-footprint tank with ample open water and complex rockwork is generally advised. Specimens can be housed singly or in small groups when introduced simultaneously; pairs and small shoals tend to be more stable than solo adults added later to an established system. New arrivals are commonly shy and may require extra acclimation effort before they begin feeding reliably.

Reef compatibility: Like most Heniochus, this species feeds on coral polyps in the wild and has been reported to nip LPS, SPS, zoanthids, tubeworms, and the mantles of tridacnid clams. It is generally not considered reef safe and is best kept in FOWLR or fish-dominant systems.

Size: LiveAquaria lists the maximum size as 10 inches (~25 cm), while FishBase and Wikipedia both list 30 cm total length and describe it as the largest species in the genus. The 20-30 cm range used here reflects commonly traded adults biased toward the FishBase-confirmed maximum.

Tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 125 US gallons (~473 L). Given that adults reach roughly 30 cm and the species is an active swimmer requiring rich vertical relief and open swimming space, a noticeably larger tank than the retail minimum is generally advised. 700 L is used here as a cautious rounded figure appropriate for full-grown adults.

Temperature: LiveAquaria advises 22.2-25.6°C (72-78°F). FishBase records a wild surface temperature of approximately 23-29°C across the species' tropical range. The 22-26°C range used here aligns with the hobby recommendation and the cooler end of the wild range.

Wild distribution and biology per FishBase: Indo-Pacific from the Andaman Islands to Samoa, north to southern Japan and south to Rowley Shoals and New Caledonia; oviparous, forms pairs during the breeding season; IUCN status Least Concern (2009).
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source (tank size, water parameters, temperament, diet, reef safety, care level).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (30 cm TL, larger than LiveAquaria's 25 cm), native range, taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), depth range (2-250 m, typically 15-40 m), oviparous reproduction, and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2009). Designated secondary URL (fishbase.mnhn.fr) returned 403; substituted the fishbase.se record for the same species id 5591.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Heniochus singularius)
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check on maximum size (30 cm / 12 in, described as the largest species of the genus Heniochus), Indo-Pacific distribution, habitat preferences (coastal, inner and outer reef slopes with complex topography), and diet (coral polyps, benthic invertebrates, algae). Used for husbandry-adjacent corroboration only.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; forms pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding is not meaningfully documented in home aquariums.
